findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the `SupportsLinks` capability reaches `_diff_links` inside `compute_outbound_mutations` — either by adding a `links_backend: SupportsLinks | None = None` parameter to `compute_outbound_mutations` (and adding it to the `run_differs._run_differs_outbound` call site passing `backend` when `isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks)`), or by adding it as a field on `OutboundDiffConfig`; the current plan omits this threading step entirely.
evidence: Plan states: 'The backend is already in scope there (threaded for the field diff). So: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/outbound_differ.py is ADDED to Scope — it passes the SupportsLinks capability as the new 4th argument to _diff_links.'

outbound_differ.py compute_outbound_mutations signature (line 396-404): only accepts local_tickets, jira_snapshot, binding_store, config, outbound_mapper, inbound_mapper — no backend/SupportsLinks parameter.
run_differs.py:497-512: outbound_differ_mod.compute_outbound_mutations() called with outbound_mapper=backend.outbound and inbound_mapper=backend.inbound, but backend itself is not passed.
No existing mechanism threads SupportsLinks into compute_outbound_mutations or _diff_links.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the backend is already in scope there (threaded for the field diff)' to justify that outbound_differ.py only needs to pass the SupportsLinks capability as a new 4th argument to _diff_links — but compute_outbound_mutations() does not currently accept a backend or SupportsLinks parameter. The backend lives in run_differs._run_differs_outbound(), which calls compute_outbound_mutations() with outbound_mapper=backend.outbound and inbound_mapper=backend.inbound, but no backend/SupportsLinks object. The plan does not specify how SupportsLinks reaches compute_outbound_mutations (new parameter, new OutboundDiffConfig field, or some other mechanism), making the threading path unbacked and leaving a scope gap.
findings.location: Plan-review remediation section, finding 1 / Scope: outbound_differ.py
scenarios: The implementation author follows the plan literally, adds SupportsLinks as a 4th arg to _diff_links, but has no way to supply it from compute_outbound_mutations because compute_outbound_mutations never receives the backend — the call chain from run_differs doesn't thread it.
A new OutboundDiffConfig field or parameter is needed but the plan leaves this unspecified, causing the implementer to discover the gap mid-implementation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `links_backend: SupportsLinks | None = None` as a keyword-only parameter to `compute_outbound_mutations`, thread it through to `_diff_links` as the new 4th argument, and update `run_differs._run_differs_outbound` to pass `backend if isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks) else None`. Add `outbound_differ.py` to the Scope section with this specific change called out.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the contradictory roles of `opaque_vendor_type`: revise the Approach section to explicitly state it is used BOTH for ADD dedup matching (direction-agnostic) AND REMOVE payload construction, and update AC2 to reflect that vendor type names serve as direction-agnostic matching keys in core (not purely opaque passengers).
evidence: Approach: 'The opaque_vendor_type passenger is carried untouched by core solely for the REMOVE payload's type key.'
Remediation §2: 'the core ADD dedup matches a local dep against a canonical entry on (opaque_vendor_type, remote_key) — NOT on (relation, remote_key)'
AC2: 'vendor link-type names appear in core solely as opaque payload passengers'
findings.finding: The Approach section characterizes `opaque_vendor_type` as carried by core 'solely for the REMOVE payload's `type` key', but the Plan-review remediation section repurposes it as the ADD dedup matching key ('the core ADD dedup matches a local dep against a canonical entry on `(opaque_vendor_type, remote_key)` — NOT on `(relation, remote_key)`'). These two sections give contradictory roles to `opaque_vendor_type`, and AC2 ('vendor link-type names appear in core solely as opaque payload passengers') is directly contradicted by the remediation's dedup mechanism where vendor type names are used as an active comparison key in core.
findings.location: Approach section ('opaque_vendor_type passenger is carried untouched by core solely for the REMOVE payload') vs. Plan-review remediation §2 ('the core ADD dedup matches … on (opaque_vendor_type, remote_key)'); AC2
scenarios: An implementer following the Approach first writes an ADD dedup that matches on (relation, remote_key), then discovers the remediation section requires (opaque_vendor_type, remote_key) — two incompatible implementations.
AC2, read literally, fails the intended implementation: the dedup comparison in core IS a use of the vendor type name, not merely opaque passthrough.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, replace 'carried untouched by core solely for the REMOVE payload's type key' with 'used by core as a direction-agnostic ADD dedup key (matching against (opaque_vendor_type, remote_key)) and carried into the REMOVE payload's type key — it is never decoded or mapped by core, only compared and forwarded.' Update AC2 to: 'Vendor link-type names are never translated in core — they are compared opaquely for ADD dedup and forwarded opaquely in REMOVE payloads; core never calls any adapter map to decode them.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the Approach and Remediation descriptions of `opaque_vendor_type`: update the Approach to state that `opaque_vendor_type` serves BOTH the ADD dedup (as the direction-agnostic match key) AND the REMOVE payload's `type` key, so both consumers are specified in one place.
evidence: Approach: 'The `opaque_vendor_type` passenger is carried untouched by core solely for the REMOVE payload's `type` key.'

Remediation item 2: 'the core ADD dedup matches a local dep against a canonical entry on `(opaque_vendor_type, remote_key)` — the direction-agnostic pair equal to today's `(type_name, other_key)` — NOT on `(relation, remote_key)`'

Current code: outbound_links.py:95-116 — key = (jira_type, target_key) used for both dedup and emitted.add; if implementer uses (relation, remote_key) instead, test_outbound_diff_links_dedup_is_direction_agnostic would fail
findings.finding: The Approach section states `opaque_vendor_type` is carried 'solely for the REMOVE payload's `type` key', but the Plan-review remediation (item 2) repurposes it as the primary key in the ADD dedup (`(opaque_vendor_type, remote_key)`). An implementer reading the Approach first and building the canonical entry tuple as a `(relation, remote_key, opaque_vendor_type)` namedtuple with `opaque_vendor_type` documented as 'REMOVE-only' may not expose it for ADD-dedup access or may confuse the field's dual role, producing a dedup that matches on `(relation, remote_key)` instead — which would break direction-agnostic dedup and re-introduce churn.
findings.location: Approach section (map_remote_links paragraph) vs. Plan-review remediation item 2
scenarios: Implementer reads Approach first, treats opaque_vendor_type as REMOVE-only, builds ADD dedup on (relation, remote_key) — a local blocks dep against an inward Blocks remote link emits a spurious ADD (direction-agnostic dedup broken).
Implementer exposes opaque_vendor_type in the canonical tuple but the Protocol docstring says 'REMOVE-only', causing a future maintainer to remove it from the ADD path.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach's `map_remote_links` paragraph, replace 'solely for the REMOVE payload's `type` key' with 'for two consumers: (a) the ADD dedup — core matches `(opaque_vendor_type, remote_key)` direction-agnostically; (b) the REMOVE payload's `type` key.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Approach whether `_diff_link_removals` continues to traverse raw `jira_fields` via `resolve_inbound_link` (permitted since `link_direction.py` is core, not `adapters.jira`) or transitions to the canonical set — and if the latter, specify that `map_remote_links` must return one canonical entry PER DIRECTION (not one per `(vendor_type, remote_key)`) so that direction-specific REMOVE matching is not collapsed.
evidence: outbound_links.py:138-199: _diff_link_removals iterates issuelinks, calls resolve_inbound_link(link) to get (other_key, relation), then matches on (relation, local_target) in local_deps
Approach: 'map_remote_links still applies today's direction-agnostic dedup internally (it may return one canonical entry per (vendor_type, remote_key))'

If a Blocks link appears as inwardIssue, resolve_inbound_link returns depends_on; if outwardIssue, returns blocks. Collapsing to one canonical entry per (vendor_type, remote_key) loses this directional disambiguation for REMOVE.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens to `_diff_link_removals` with respect to raw `jira_fields` traversal. The Approach says '_diff_links/_diff_link_removals compare local ticket["deps"] against the canonical set in relation vocabulary' (implying both functions switch to the canonical set), but `_diff_link_removals` currently calls `resolve_inbound_link` from `link_direction.py` (a core module — no adapter import) to direction-disambiguate each link for the managed-ref REMOVE gate. If the plan means `_diff_link_removals` also switches to the canonical set from `map_remote_links`, it needs the `relation` field per canonical entry, which `map_remote_links` does provide. But if `map_remote_links` does internal dedup (one entry per `(vendor_type, remote_key)`), it may collapse two direction-distinct links (e.g. blocks vs. depends_on for the SAME Blocks link) into one canonical entry with one `relation`, losing the directional information the REMOVE gate needs. The plan does not resolve this.
findings.location: Approach section — '_diff_links/_diff_link_removals... compare local ticket["deps"] against the canonical set' + 'map_remote_links applies today's direction-agnostic dedup internally (it may return one canonical entry per (vendor_type, remote_key))'
scenarios: Local ticket has no 'depends_on' dep for key K; Jira has an inward Blocks link from K (i.e. local depends_on K). The canonical entry has relation=blocks (outward) due to dedup collision. REMOVE gate checks (relation=blocks, K) against local_deps, finds no match, emits spurious REMOVE.

Alternatively: implementer keeps _diff_link_removals traversing raw jira_fields directly (no change to REMOVE path), which is fine for the adapters.jira AC but contradicts the plan's 'canonical set' language — leaving an ambiguity the implementer must resolve by guessing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'code review' in AC2 with a concrete deterministic check — e.g., extend the AST neutrality gate to assert that `_diff_links` / `_diff_link_removals` in `outbound_links.py` contain no string literals matching Jira link-type names (e.g. `grep -E '"(Blocks|Cloners|Duplicate)"' src/.../outbound_links.py` exits non-zero), or alternatively rely solely on the behavior tests and remove the code-review clause.
evidence: 'verified by code review + the behavior tests in Testing' — 'code review' is not a deterministic command
The behavior tests are referenced but no pytest selector or file path is given to anchor the claim
AC1 and AC4 each have a concrete, named gate (AST test, held-out test); AC2 does not match that specificity
findings.finding: AC2 names 'code review' as one of its proving mechanisms, which is a subjective, non-deterministic check — not a concrete proving command that produces binary evidence.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'Link diffing compares canonical relations only; vendor link-type names appear in core solely as opaque payload passengers — verified by code review + the behavior tests in Testing'
scenarios: An agent marks AC2 done after writing the behavior tests without verifying that vendor type names are truly absent from core comparison logic — the code-review gate is unenforced
CI passes (behavior tests green) but core still compares on vendor names via a path not covered by the named tests

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the contract for unknown relations explicitly in the `link_payload_for_relation` bullet (e.g. 'returns `None` for relations with no vendor mapping; core skips ADD emission for that dep, preserving today's silent-skip semantics').
evidence: Plan says 'link_payload_for_relation(relation) → (vendor_type, swap) — the emission translation, replacing the inline _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK.get(relation)'
The `.get()` idiom returns None for unknown keys; what core does with a None return from the port method is unspecified
No AC or Testing bullet covers the behavior when a local dep has an unmapped relation
findings.finding: The plan does not state what `link_payload_for_relation` must return (or what core must do) when `relation` is not in the adapter's map — today's inline `.get()` silently returns `None` and the dep is skipped, but the plan leaves this edge-case contract implicit.
scenarios: Implementer raises KeyError instead of returning None for unknown relations, breaking today's silent-skip semantics
Implementer returns None but core doesn't guard against it, causing an unpack error on `(vendor_type, swap)`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the bullet: 'Returns `None` when the relation has no vendor mapping; callers must guard `if result is None: continue` before unpacking, preserving today's `.get()` skip behavior.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `JiraBackend.map_remote_links` reuses `link_direction.JIRA_LINK_TO_RELATION` (the existing SSOT for Jira-type-name → canonical relation used by `resolve_inbound_link`) rather than deriving an independent reverse of `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`, keeping a single source of truth shared by the ADD-dedup and REMOVE paths.
evidence: Plan phrase: 'translating vendor link-type names back to canonical relations via the adapter's own map'

link_direction.py:19: JIRA_LINK_TO_RELATION: dict[str, str] = {"Blocks": "blocks", "Relates": "relates_to"} — this is already the definitive reverse map, used by resolve_inbound_link which is imported by outbound_links._diff_link_removals
jira_fields.py:79-83: _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK maps relation→(Jira type, swap); inverting it mechanically yields a partial reverse map that duplicates JIRA_LINK_TO_RELATION
ABSENCE: the plan does not specify whether the JiraBackend.map_remote_links implementation will consume link_direction.JIRA_LINK_TO_RELATION (the existing SSOT) or derive its own reverse
findings.finding: The plan's map_remote_links Jira implementation will need to translate Jira link-type names (e.g. 'Blocks', 'Relates') back to canonical rebar relations, but link_direction.JIRA_LINK_TO_RELATION already provides exactly this reverse map in a core module. The plan says the adapter will translate 'via the adapter's own map' without stating whether it reuses link_direction.JIRA_LINK_TO_RELATION or re-derives the reverse from _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK — if the latter, two independent sources of truth for Jira-type-name→relation will exist and can diverge when a new link type is added.
findings.location: Approach section — 'map_remote_links … translating vendor link-type names back to canonical relations via the adapter's own map'; also src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/link_direction.py:19 (JIRA_LINK_TO_RELATION) and adapters/jira/jira_fields.py:79 (_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK)
scenarios: A future contributor adds 'Mentions' to _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK but forgets to update a hand-derived reverse map inside map_remote_links — remote links of the new type silently get relation=None and are never matched or removed.

The two maps drift; a link type mapped differently in JIRA_LINK_TO_RELATION vs the adapter's internal reverse causes the ADD-dedup and REMOVE paths to disagree on which canonical relation a given Jira link type represents.
findings.suggested_fix: In the JiraBackend.map_remote_links implementation, import and use link_direction.JIRA_LINK_TO_RELATION for the type-name→relation lookup, exactly as resolve_inbound_link does; do not re-derive this map from _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK. Call out this reuse explicitly in the Approach section so the implementor does not accidentally create a second map.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a docstring to `map_remote_links` (and/or update ADR 0035 / AGENTS.md) capturing the `opaque_vendor_type` passenger contract and the requirement that ADD dedup matches on `(opaque_vendor_type, remote_key)` — not `(relation, remote_key)` — so future adapter implementers have a normative spec to follow.
evidence: 'the core ADD dedup matches a local dep against a canonical entry on (opaque_vendor_type, remote_key) — the direction-agnostic pair … NOT on (relation, remote_key)'
'The opaque_vendor_type passenger is carried untouched by core solely for the REMOVE payload's type key'
ADR 0035 §(d) is cited as governing but no update to it is mentioned
No ADR/AGENTS.md/design-doc update appears in Scope
findings.finding: The `opaque_vendor_type` passenger pattern and the ADD-dedup matching rule (`(opaque_vendor_type, remote_key)` not `(relation, remote_key)`) are non-obvious protocol contracts on `SupportsLinks` that any future non-Jira adapter implementer must follow exactly — but no ADR update, protocol docstring spec, or AGENTS.md entry is planned to capture this contract.
scenarios: A future adapter author implementing `map_remote_links` for a non-Jira backend returns canonical entries keyed by relation rather than vendor type, silently breaking direction-agnostic ADD dedup
A reviewer reading ADR 0035 sees the canonical-shape rule but does not find the passenger-pattern exception that permits vendor type to cross core for dedup purposes
findings.suggested_fix: In `_backend.py`, add a docstring to `map_remote_links` stating: 'Each returned entry carries an `opaque_vendor_type` field whose value is the backend-native link-type identifier. Core uses `(opaque_vendor_type, remote_key)` — not `(relation, remote_key)` — as the direction-agnostic ADD-dedup key. Implementors MUST populate this field consistently with `link_payload_for_relation`'s returned vendor_type.' Reference this in ADR 0035 §(d) as the permitted opaque-passenger exception.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add removal of the `_existing_jira_links` re-export from `outbound_differ.py:73` to Scope (drop or replace the `# noqa: F401` re-export once the function moves to the adapter).
evidence:
outbound_differ.py:71-74: `from rebar_reconciler.outbound_links import (  # noqa: F401
    _diff_links,
    _existing_jira_links,
)`
Approach section: '_existing_jira_links … The Jira implementation absorbs today's `_existing_jira_links`' — implying the function moves out of outbound_links.py
Plan Scope lists `outbound_differ.py` only for 'passes the SupportsLinks capability as the new 4th argument to `_diff_links`', not for removing the `_existing_jira_links` re-export
findings.finding: `outbound_differ.py` re-exports `_existing_jira_links` at line 73 (`from rebar_reconciler.outbound_links import (_diff_links, _existing_jira_links)`). Once this plan removes `_existing_jira_links` from `outbound_links.py` (replacing it with `map_remote_links` on the adapter), this module-level import raises `ImportError` and the entire `outbound_differ` module fails to load. The plan does not mention removing or replacing this re-export.
scenarios: After `_existing_jira_links` is removed from `outbound_links.py`, the import in `outbound_differ.py:73` raises `ImportError`, breaking every test and production path that imports `outbound_differ`.
findings.suggested_fix: In the `outbound_differ.py` Scope description, explicitly state: remove `_existing_jira_links` from the `from rebar_reconciler.outbound_links import` line; if external callers rely on `outbound_differ._existing_jira_links`, document the replacement symbol.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Approach whether `map_remote_links`'s `remote_fields` argument is the full Jira `fields` dict or just the `issuelinks` list (e.g. 'accepts `raw_fields: dict` — the full vendor fields blob — and reads `raw_fields["issuelinks"]` internally').
evidence: Plan says 'map_remote_links(remote_fields) → the canonical link set' without specifying what `remote_fields` is
Existing code at outbound_links.py:38-63 reads raw vendor `fields.issuelinks`, but the plan does not state whether the new method receives the full `fields` dict or only the `issuelinks` sub-list
The SupportsLinks protocol definition in _backend.py depends on this choice
findings.finding: The parameter shape of `map_remote_links(remote_fields)` is unspecified — an implementing agent must decide whether `remote_fields` is the full Jira `fields` dict or only the `issuelinks` list, a real design choice the plan leaves open.
scenarios: Implementer defines `map_remote_links(issuelinks_list)` while the call site passes the full `fields` dict (or vice versa), causing a runtime KeyError or type mismatch
Protocol is defined one way in _backend.py and called another way in outbound_links.py, creating a latent bug invisible to the AST gate
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the `map_remote_links` bullet: 'The argument is the full vendor `fields` dict (same object passed to other field-translation methods); the implementation reads `raw_fields.get("issuelinks", [])` internally.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge whether other implementations of `SupportsLinks` exist and, if so, describe an expand-contract sequence (e.g., add methods with default `NotImplemented` raises or abstract stubs first) or a rollback path; if the Jira adapter is the only implementation, state that explicitly to close the gap.
evidence: Scope names only `adapters/jira/backend.py` as implementing the two new members, but `_backend.py` defines the shared protocol used by all backends.
No expand-contract sequence (e.g., add methods with defaults first, then make mandatory) is described.
No rollback path is stated if the protocol extension causes runtime failures in non-Jira backends.
findings.finding: The plan adds two new mandatory members (`map_remote_links`, `link_payload_for_relation`) to the `SupportsLinks` capability/protocol without describing an expand-contract sequence or rollback path for other existing implementations of that protocol. If any backend beyond the Jira adapter already implements `SupportsLinks`, those implementations will break at the protocol boundary with no migration guidance.
findings.location: Scope: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py`; Approach: 'Move both link translations onto the SupportsLinks capability'
scenarios: A second backend (e.g., a mock or test double) implementing SupportsLinks lacks the new methods and raises AttributeError at runtime, discovered only at integration-test time with no documented rollback.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line statement to the Approach: 'The Jira adapter is the only current implementation of SupportsLinks; the protocol extension is therefore non-breaking in practice. Any test doubles will be updated in the same PR.' If other implementations exist, add: 'Expand-contract: add the two methods as abstract on the protocol class in one commit; update all implementations before removing the abstract default.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test case covering `link_payload_for_relation(relation)` returning `None` (unmapped local relation): verify the ADD path skips the dep silently and emits no ADD mutation, mirroring today's `.get(relation)` guard semantics.
evidence: Plan Testing: 'Happy path: a local dep missing remotely emits an ADD with today's exact payload keys'
Plan Testing: no test case for link_payload_for_relation returning None
Plan Approach: 'link_payload_for_relation(relation) → (vendor_type, swap) — the emission translation, replacing the inline _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK.get(relation) at outbound_links.py:105'
ABSENCE: no edge-case test for an unmapped local relation
findings.finding: The Testing section covers the `link_payload_for_relation(relation)` happy path (known relation → vendor_type + swap) but specifies no test for the failure path where `link_payload_for_relation` returns None (i.e., a local dep carries a relation the adapter has no mapping for). Today's inline `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK.get(relation)` returning None is guarded somewhere in the existing diff loop; after the refactor, that guard must still exist but is neither described in the plan nor verified by a test case, leaving the behavior (silent skip vs. raise) unspecified and untested.
findings.location: Testing section / outbound_links.py ADD path (outbound_links.py:105 area)
scenarios: A ticket with a locally declared dep whose relation string has no entry in the adapter's map (e.g. a future relation, a typo, or a test fixture with an unknown relation) hits the refactored ADD path and either silently drops the dep or raises unexpectedly — neither behavior is pinned by a test, so regressions in this guard go undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: In the link-diff behavior tests, add a scenario where a local dep has an unknown relation (not in the adapter's map). Assert: `_diff_links` (or `compute_outbound_mutations`) emits zero ADD mutations for that dep, and no exception is raised. This pins the existing guard's behavior through the refactor.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_link_dedup.py` to Scope and specify how its four `_diff_links(ticket, jira_fields, binding)` call sites are updated (e.g., pass a stub `SupportsLinks` or restructure the fixture).
evidence: outbound_differ._diff_links(ticket, jira_fields, binding) at test_link_dedup.py:98
outbound_differ._diff_links(ticket, {'issuelinks': []}, binding) at test_link_dedup.py:109,132
outbound_differ._diff_links(ticket, jira_fields, binding) == [] at test_link_dedup.py:166
Plan Scope lists `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/` for new link-diff behavior tests but does not enumerate updating this existing file
findings.finding: `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_link_dedup.py` calls `outbound_differ._diff_links(ticket, jira_fields, binding)` with 3 positional arguments at lines 98, 109, 132, and 166. When the plan adds a 4th `SupportsLinks` argument to `_diff_links`, all four call sites break. This file is not listed in Scope.
scenarios: After the plan adds a 4th SupportsLinks argument to `_diff_links`, the four direct call sites in test_link_dedup.py raise TypeError (wrong number of arguments), breaking CI.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_link_dedup.py` to Scope; in that file replace direct `_diff_links(ticket, jira_fields, binding)` calls with a stub backend that implements `SupportsLinks.map_remote_links` / `link_payload_for_relation`, or pass the backend as the 4th arg.

findings.finding: The plan relocates _existing_jira_links + relation translation adapter-side and has core _diff_links compare against a canonical set from SupportsLinks.map_remote_links, but its Scope omits outbound_differ.py and run_differs.py — the sites that must be edited to obtain the SupportsLinks capability and thread it (plus the canonical set) into _diff_links, and to drop outbound_differ.py's _existing_jira_links re-export.
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror sibling 625b: thread the SupportsLinks capability from run_differs (backend is already in scope) into compute_outbound_mutations as a keyword param, forward it through _compute_outbound_update_mutation to _diff_links/_diff_link_removals, and default it to None resolving via the neutral select_backend seam for direct-call test sites; drop the _existing_jira_links re-export.

findings.finding: The proposed `map_remote_links` mechanism loses the existing direction-agnostic ADD dedup. The current `_existing_jira_links` inserts `(jira_type_name, other_key)` for BOTH `inwardIssue` and `outwardIssue` sides (lines 57-62), so any `Blocks` link to key Y appears as `("Blocks", Y)` regardless of which endpoint the local issue occupies. The refactored ADD dedup would compare a local `"blocks"` dep against a canonical set where the same physical Jira link appears as EITHER `("blocks", Y)` OR `("depends_on", Y)` — not both — so a local `blocks Y` dep will fail the dedup check when the Jira link was recorded in the opposite direction (`inwardIssue`), emitting a spurious ADD every pass.
findings.suggested_fix: In the `map_remote_links` spec, state explicitly: for each Jira issuelink of a mapped asymmetric type (e.g. `Blocks`), insert entries for BOTH canonical relations against the same `remote_key` — i.e. both `("blocks", Y, "Blocks")` and `("depends_on", Y, "Blocks")`. The `_diff_links` ADD dedup then checks `(relation, target_key)` against this set and correctly matches regardless of which end the local issue occupies. The REMOVE path is unaffected as it already uses `resolve_inbound_link`'s single directional result for managed-ref matching.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
